title: Making a mini swale to slow and sink rainwater
subtitle: A simple on-contour trench that turns runoff into ground infiltration
excerpt: A swale is a level trench dug on the contour of a slope. Rainwater running down the slope fills the trench and infiltrates into the ground rather than washing away. A mini swale 200-300 mm wide and 150 mm deep is enough to noticeably reduce runoff from a garden slope and recharge soil moisture during heavy rain.
sourceNotes: Permaculture Research Institute guidance on swale design and placement. Brad Lancaster 'Rainwater Harvesting for Drylands and Beyond' Vol 1. RHS guidance on managing excess water in the garden.

glossaryTerms.definition: A level, on-contour trench designed to capture and infiltrate rainwater. Unlike a drainage channel (which moves water away), a swale holds water until it infiltrates into the surrounding soil. Named after the permaculture design principle of slowing water movement through landscapes.

Following the contour of the land at a consistent elevation, so that a trench or path dug on contour is level across its full length. Digging a swale on contour ensures water spreads evenly along the trench rather than draining to one end.

content.text: works only if it is dug

: that is, completely level across its length. A trench that tilts even slightly will have water running to the low end and overflowing there. Use a long spirit level (or a water level made from a garden hose) to mark the contour line before digging.

Start at one point on the slope. Using a long spirit level held at ground level, move along the slope adjusting height until the bubble centres, then mark that point with a cane. Repeat every 500-800 mm across the slope to establish the contour line. Connect the canes with string before digging. A 1-2% error across a 3-metre swale will cause water to pile up at one end; re-check each cane position before you commit to digging.

Dig the trench along the contour line: 200-300 mm wide, 150-200 mm deep. Pile the spoil on the downhill side of the trench to form a small berm, which slows water further and provides a raised planting area above the swale. On a gentle slope (less than 10°) a single swale may be enough. On a steeper slope, consider two or three swales at different elevations, each catching overflow from the one above.

An unplanted swale erodes and silts up. Plant the berm (spoil pile) with perennials or shrubs to stabilise it. Line the swale itself with a layer of wood chip or gravel to slow silting. Moisture-tolerant plants like willows, dogwood, or ornamental grasses can be planted in the swale base: they tolerate periodic waterlogging and their roots improve infiltration over time.

Do not dig a swale close to a building foundation. Water infiltrating near foundations can cause subsidence in clay soils. Keep any swale at least 3 metres from the building footprint. On heavy clay where infiltration is very slow, a swale may flood and overflow rather than infiltrating: test infiltration first by filling a test hole with water and timing how quickly it drains.
